user_query:
I keep locking myself out, and I’m trying to get organized before a little safety chat with my building and to message my helpful neighbors. Using the files under input/, please do the following and save all results under outputs/:

1) Summarize my lockout incidents into a table saved as outputs/lockout_summary.csv with these exact columns:
   - weekday: full weekday name computed from the date
   - time_block: one of morning (05:00–11:59), afternoon (12:00–16:59), evening (17:00–21:59), night (22:00–04:59)
   - incidents: number of incidents in the (weekday, time_block) bucket
   - count_keyholder: count where unlock_method is one of [neighbor_spare_key, family_member, management, landlord]
   - count_locksmith: count where unlock_method is locksmith
   - count_other: count of remaining rows in the bucket
   - avg_response_minutes: average of response_minutes for the bucket, rounded to the nearest whole number
   Use input/incidents.csv for this. If who_helped is missing or not in contacts.csv, still include the incident in the correct counts based on unlock_method.

2) Inspect the code and configuration to audit who would actually be notified. Specifically, look at notify.py to determine which YAML key it uses for recipients (do not assume based only on the YAML). Then cross-check those contact IDs against input/contacts.csv and write outputs/mismatched_recipients.csv with columns:
   - contact_id: the ID from the config key that notify.py actually uses
   - in_config_key: the name of the config key from alert_config.yaml that notify.py uses (as determined by reading notify.py)
   - exists_in_contacts: yes or no
   - detail: if no, say "not found in contacts.csv"; if yes, provide the contact’s relationship from contacts.csv
   Use both input/alert_config.yaml and notify.py for this step, and make your audit based on what the code really reads.

3) Prepare meeting notes with clear action items saved as outputs/meeting_notes.md. Include the following sections:
   - Summary: total incidents, and a brief note on which time_block(s) and weekday(s) appear most frequent from outputs/lockout_summary.csv
   - Findings: at least 3 bullet points, including whether any incidents occurred during quiet_hours from input/alert_config.yaml and a note about who typically helps (from incidents joined to contacts when who_helped matches contact_id)
   - Action Items: at least 4 concrete items derived from the data and the config audit (e.g., if many night incidents occur, propose a keypad or confirm off-hours contacts; if any recipients are mismatched, propose fixes). For each item, assign an owner by contact_id from contacts.csv when appropriate, otherwise "TBD".
   - References: mention the files used (input/incidents.csv, input/contacts.csv, input/alert_config.yaml, notify.py) and point to outputs/mismatched_recipients.csv.

4) Draft a plain-text email for my neighbors and save it as outputs/email_to_neighbors.txt with:
   - A subject line: "Please confirm your spare key details"
   - A short paragraph summarizing how often I’ve been locked out recently (use counts you computed) and that I value their help
   - A request for the neighbors in contacts.csv (relationship == neighbor) to confirm their phone and availability; list each neighbor’s current name, phone, and availability exactly as in contacts.csv
   - A polite note about quiet_hours from input/alert_config.yaml (start and end times) and what to do if I call during those hours
   Don’t send anything; just draft the message content.

Inputs are in the repository:
- input/incidents.csv: my recent lockouts
- input/contacts.csv: neighbors, family, manager, locksmith
- input/alert_config.yaml: notification settings (quiet_hours, recipients, groups)
- notify.py: the script my grandson set up; please read it to know which recipients list is actually used

Please create the outputs folder if it doesn’t exist and ensure each deliverable is written to the exact paths above. Thank you. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
